source_book: The Book of Secrets (Vigyan Bhairav Tantra) by Osho
description: Faithful, paraphrased summaries (in original phrasing of the writer of this file) of the opening / framing sections of the 39 sutra-introducing chapters in Osho's discourses on Vigyan Bhairav Tantra. Each entry captures the frame of mind Osho asks the reader to bring to the techniques in that chapter. One short attributed direct quote (each under 25 words) is included per chapter.

chapters.title: Breath - A Bridge to the Universe
chapters.summary: Before practising any breath technique, Osho asks you to drop the seeker's mistake. Truth is not a future goal - it is already the case, and you already are it. The mind, however, only operates in past or future; the present has no room for it to move. So as long as you are seeking - even seeking liberation or no-desire - you keep missing what is already here. Shiva therefore gives techniques without any philosophical preamble, because the mind would only turn the philosophy into another goal. The four breath sutras are simple turnings of attention back into this moment. As Osho puts it: "You cannot seek truth. You can find it, but you cannot seek it. The very seeking is the hindrance."
chapters.quote: You cannot seek truth. You can find it, but you cannot seek it. The very seeking is the hindrance.

chapters.title: Five Techniques of Attentiveness
chapters.summary: Osho opens with Pythagoras, who was refused entry to an Egyptian mystery school until he completed forty days of fasting and conscious breathing. Afterwards he said his centre had moved from intellect to feeling - from concept to actual experience. The same Indian method now appears in Shiva's first sutra: focus attention between the eyebrows, on the dormant third eye (the pineal gland). Osho says this point is magnetic - it pulls attention into itself effortlessly. Once attention settles there, you stop being identified with anger or desire and start watching thoughts pass like clouds in the sky. As he puts it: "For the third eye, attention is food. It has been hungry for lives and lives."
chapters.quote: For the third eye, attention is food. It has been hungry for lives and lives.

chapters.title: Techniques to Put You at Ease
chapters.summary: Osho frames these techniques by saying we all have a centre but live off it, and this off-centre living is the root of every tension. He sketches four types: the "normal" person with a fixed identity, who is really dead inside; the artist, liquid and swinging between madness and ecstasy; the permanently mad, gone outside completely; and the Buddha, who has returned home. The Buddha still eats, sleeps and feels heat, but always in this moment, with no projection into tomorrow. Wherever there is becoming, there is tension; wherever there is pure being, there is ease. As Osho bluntly notes: "The normal man is really a dead man, living between these two points."
chapters.quote: The normal man is really a dead man, living between these two points.

chapters.title: Techniques for Centering
chapters.summary: Osho says we are all born with a centre but completely forget it. We can live without knowing it, but not without it. Without that knowing, life feels rootless - what Sartre called "thrownness," the alien sense that one has been hurled into a foreign world. If you live as thrown, anguish follows; if you sense yourself as grown organically, like a tree, bliss follows. A child first lives rooted in the navel (the hara), then drifts to the heart, then to the head, losing the original ground. These centering sutras are devices to take consciousness back. As Osho puts it: "Bliss is the result of an organic unity with the universe, and anguish the result of an enmity."
chapters.quote: Bliss is the result of an organic unity with the universe, and anguish the result of an enmity.

chapters.title: Techniques to Penetrate the Inner
chapters.summary: Osho diagnoses the basic human condition as living on the circumference of a circle that has no centre. We have a "without" but no real "within" - even when physically alone, we are inwardly crowded with images of other people. So all our talk of going inside is hollow: the words have meaning only intellectually, because we have never actually been there. These three techniques - closing the senses with the hands, dropping into the heart, hovering in the middle of opposites - are devices to pierce that crowded circumference and finally enter. As Osho frames it: "Man is as if he is a circle without a centre." Until that centre is found, nothing within is real to you.
chapters.quote: Man is as if he is a circle without a centre.

chapters.title: Inner Centering
chapters.summary: Osho explains that the body has two dimensions: outward through the senses, where consciousness meets matter, and inward through centering, where the same reality appears as non-matter. The seer is always behind the eyes; close the senses and the seer remains. But the inner centre can never be approached head-on. Like happiness in play or music, it is always a by-product - appearing only when you forget the goal and absorb yourself in some other act. These techniques therefore never tell you to seek the centre; they ask you to look lovingly at an object, sit on the buttocks, sway gently - and let the centre arrive on its own. "Whatsoever is beautiful, whatsoever is eternal is so delicate that if you try to grab it directly it is destroyed."
chapters.quote: Whatsoever is beautiful, whatsoever is eternal is so delicate that if you try to grab it directly it is destroyed.

chapters.title: Seeing the Past as a Dream
chapters.summary: Osho borrows from Gurdjieff that identification is the only real sin - and these sutras attack it directly. We were children once and are no longer, yet we still merge with the memory rather than watching it like a film. The move, Osho says, is to begin with the past: see your childhood as a dream and you suddenly notice that what is present today will also become a dream tomorrow. The witness itself is never inside time. He cites Rousseau's Confessions as a man who admitted every sin except being identified with them. As Osho puts it: "Anything that you can look at as a witness is a dream." These techniques train that capacity to step out of the film.
chapters.quote: Anything that you can look at as a witness is a dream.

chapters.title: Several Stop Techniques
chapters.summary: Osho splits life into two balances - being and doing. Being is already the case; doing and having are achievements piled around it like a circumference. Most people live entirely on that circumference and never touch the centre. These "stop" sutras attack it directly. Gurdjieff borrowed the same method from Tibetan lamas (who got it from Vigyan Bhairav): in the middle of dance, freeze. The body still has momentum to continue, but you don't cooperate. In that sudden gap, the body and the watcher inside it split apart, and for an instant you actually see yourself. The smallest adjustment ruins it. As Osho puts it: "Whatsoever you have is not your being, and whatsoever you do is not your being."
chapters.quote: Whatsoever you have is not your being, and whatsoever you do is not your being.

chapters.title: A Technique for the Intellectual and a Technique for the Feeling Type
chapters.summary: Tantra's diagnosis here is radical: the mind is not sick - the mind is the sickness. Western psychology assumes the average mind is healthy and only treats deviations from the norm; it tries to readjust the genius back to average. Osho says this misses the point - the average mind is itself "normally ill," just commonly enough that no one notices. The body, since it is always dying, can never be perfectly healthy; only relatively. The mind is the same: structurally a bridge between matter and spirit, dying and deathless, so it is bound to be tense. Curing it from inside is impossible. The only move is transcendence. As Osho puts it: "It is not that you are tense within, but rather, you are the tension."
chapters.quote: It is not that you are tense within, but rather, you are the tension.

chapters.title: Three Looking Techniques
chapters.summary: Before these three sutras Osho lingers on the eyes themselves. Of all the body, the eyes are the least bodily - a meeting point where matter comes closest to the consciousness behind it. The hand, the heart, no other organ comes that near. Because of that proximity, a single attentive jump from the eyes can land you at the source. These techniques use that bridge: closing the eyes to see your inner being in detail, looking at a bowl without registering the sides or material, or seeing an ordinary face as if for the very first time. The point is not what you look at but the quality of the looking. As Osho says: "A single jump from the eyes can lead you to the source."
chapters.quote: A single jump from the eyes can lead you to the source.

chapters.title: Several More Looking Methods
chapters.summary: Osho sets up a clean diagram before these sutras: consciousness sits at the centre, the senses sit on the boundary, and objects lie beyond it. The senses are a doorway that opens both ways - outward to the world, or inward to the centre - and the distance is the same. Quoting Zen master Bokuju, he says nirvana is no farther away than the marketplace. We turn outward only because bodily needs pull us out; nothing yet pulls us in. The need to move inward must first be created, and Osho says it is born from one thing - the awareness of one's own death. Animals see others die; only a human can see himself dying. As he puts it: "If you are not aware of death, you have not yet become man."
chapters.quote: If you are not aware of death, you have not yet become man.

chapters.title: From Words to Pure Words to Being
chapters.summary: Osho opens by noting that Sartre titled his autobiography simply "Words" - and that, he says, is the autobiography of every human being. We are stuffed with words from waking to sleeping. The self, however, lives below, behind, around the mind - never inside it. The mistake is identification: we believe we are the mind, and that single error is the root of every misery. Why do we identify so deeply? Because thinking has been the human survival weapon, against animals, nature, and other people. The proof is simple - call someone's body ill and they shrug; call their mind ill and they take offense. These sound techniques begin to unhook us from words. "You are filled with words, and this process of words continues the whole day, even in the mind."
chapters.quote: You are filled with words, and this process of words continues the whole day, even in the mind.

chapters.title: Soundlessness, Soundfulness and Total Awareness
chapters.summary: Osho threads modern physics into Tantra here. Just as physicists now postulate anti-matter - a hole of nothingness behind every dense object - Tantra has always said anti-sound, silence, sits just behind every sound. They are two faces of one coin. Sound can therefore be used as a doorway into soundlessness, mind into no-mind. He notes the two schools: Sankhya (and J. Krishnamurti) insists mind cannot be used at all, only seen through; Yoga and Tantra insist mind must be used - but negatively, as a springboard that weakens itself. These chants of "Aum" work on the second principle. As Osho puts it: "Wherever there is sound, just behind it there is silence." The technique slips you across that thin line.
chapters.quote: Wherever there is sound, just behind it there is silence.

chapters.title: Methods for the Dropping of Mind
chapters.summary: Tantra, Osho says, refuses the religious split between this world (sansara) and liberation (moksha). The two are not contradictory; the ultimate is hidden inside the immediate, here and now. The mind, however, is a prism: it divides whatever passes through it, and it cannot do otherwise - that is its nature. So a no-mind approach is needed to perceive wholeness. Because Tantra denies division, total acceptance becomes possible, and acceptance alone produces real relaxation. Religions that brand the world or the body as sin only generate guilt, neurosis and seriousness. Osho says every religion has an outer life-denying face and an inner esoteric core that, when healthy, is always tantric. "Unless you accept the world totally, you cannot be at ease within."
chapters.quote: Unless you accept the world totally, you cannot be at ease within.

chapters.title: From Sound to Inner Silence
chapters.summary: Osho sharpens the difference between philosophy, science and Tantra here. Philosophy thinks about ultimate truth; science discovers what is. Tantra is also a science, but its territory is the inner - a subjective experiment rather than speculation. The very word tantra, he points out, means technique, and so this book contains no doctrine, only one hundred and twelve experiments. He warns against chasing the ultimate: it is hidden inside the immediate, and by reaching past the immediate you lose both. He tells the joke of a priest asking the dying Mulla Nasruddin about the Trinity - and concludes: "All philosophy is like this: it is asking puzzles while you are dying." Tantra simply gives the technique and lets you find out.
chapters.quote: All philosophy is like this: it is asking puzzles while you are dying.

chapters.title: The Spirituality of the Tantric Sex Act
chapters.summary: Freud said man is born neurotic; Osho calls this only half true. The newborn is born real, with feeling intact - society then drives the split. To be loved by his parents the child must suppress what he feels and fit a code, and slowly the feeling self is buried under the thinking self. Once split, even our love becomes head-talk. Real needs (love, contact) get replaced by symbolic ones (food, fame, attention from crowds). These tantric sutras - including the famous one on sex - work as instruments of reunion, returning a person to the feeling centre. Sex, properly approached, can throw you back into wholeness, which is why it opens this set. As Osho puts it: "Even when you say that you feel, you only think that you feel."
chapters.quote: Even when you say that you feel, you only think that you feel.

chapters.title: Turning Inward toward the Real
chapters.summary: Osho opens this set with a striking diagnosis: civilization is a training in becoming unreal. The child is born undivided - body and mind are two aspects of one being - but education imposes the split, and from then on we identify with the head and treat the body as slave or enemy. The damage runs deeper than we realise. Where, he asks, does your body actually end? If the sun stopped tomorrow, you would die today; without the air around you, you stop in seconds. So your body has no fixed boundary - it stretches to the limits of the universe. These four sutras work to dissolve the head-centred identity and let you feel that wider, real self. As Osho puts it: "Civilization is a training in how to become unreal."
chapters.quote: Civilization is a training in how to become unreal.

chapters.title: Techniques to Witness the Flux-like Film of Life
chapters.summary: Osho opens with the image: the original mind is a mirror, always pure, but covered with dust from many lifetimes of travel. The East and the West differ here. Christianity claims something happened to being itself - sin. The East says no, nothing has happened; the being remains untouched, only dust collected. So you are not condemned, only identified with the dust - your memories, knowledge and past. The capacity to know is pure; what you know is dust. Buddha left every teacher who pointed at some future liberation and finally found it by simply staying with this moment. These four techniques work the same way: they wipe the mirror, but only if used with understanding rather than mechanically. As Osho puts it: "Knowing is your purity, knowledge is dust."
chapters.quote: Knowing is your purity, knowledge is dust.

chapters.title: From the Wave to the Cosmic Ocean
chapters.summary: Quoting Sri Aurobindo - "the whole life is yoga" - Osho stresses that meditation cannot be a part-time activity. Either it has happened to you and pervades everything, or it has not. You cannot meditate for an hour in the morning and then live mechanically for the rest of the day; that, he says, isn't meditation at all, just an exercise. Meditation is more like a second breathing: just as you breathe air without effort while walking or sleeping, you start breathing consciousness once meditation has truly arrived. It becomes the quality of your being. These three sutras therefore turn ordinary moments - wandering attention, sensory clarity, the universe's own pulse - into doorways. As Osho puts it: "Either it is - and when it is you are wholly in it - or it is not."
chapters.quote: Either it is - and when it is you are wholly in it - or it is not.

chapters.title: Tantric Methods for Awareness and Non-Judgment
chapters.summary: Osho frames these techniques with a paradox: you have to attain what you already are. Sansara and nirvana, the world and liberation, are not two places but two attitudes toward the same reality. The methods can therefore be very simple. The catch is the ego: it craves difficulty, because only difficulty offers it the trophy of conquest. Anything easy feels insulting, so the mind dismisses simple techniques even when they work. Spiritual awakening is not a causal event needing time; it is more like waking from sleep - first disorientation, then sudden recognition. These eight sutras cultivate that growing alertness. As Osho puts it: "You are already the person you long to be, you are already where you want to reach."
chapters.quote: You are already the person you long to be, you are already where you want to reach.

chapters.title: Finding the Changeless through the Changing
chapters.summary: Osho draws on Northrope to contrast two minds. The Western mind hunts the "theoretical component" - the causal levers by which nature can be predicted and controlled. The Eastern mind hunts the "aesthetic component" - how to participate in nature, how to dissolve into it. He calls science a relationship of hatred and religion a relationship of love. Tantra is purely Eastern: a love effort. He gives a vivid example - when Hillary climbed Everest, Western papers said "conquered"; a Zen monastery's wall paper said "Everest has been befriended." Approached as conquest, existence shows only dead matter; approached as participation, the same existence reveals life. As Osho puts it: "If you approach it through analysis, it appears material; if you approach it through participation, it appears as life."
chapters.quote: If you approach it through analysis, it appears material; if you approach it through participation, it appears as life.

chapters.title: Remaining with the Real
chapters.summary: Osho opens with a Hui-neng question: you are alive, you are conscious - why don't you know who you are? You must have built a barrier. The good news, he says, is that nothing positive needs to be done; the barrier just has to be seen. And the barrier is the dreaming mind - the past constantly interpreting whatever happens now. When you listen, you don't really hear; you hear your own commentary. Look at a flower and the word "beautiful" steps between you and it. These two sutras - the hen mothering her chicks, the many suns reflected in water - are about settling back into reality without interpretation. "You are not hearing me; you are hearing yourself, because simultaneously you are interpreting."
chapters.quote: You are not hearing me; you are hearing yourself, because simultaneously you are interpreting.

chapters.title: Tantric Meditation Using Light
chapters.summary: Before these light techniques Osho redraws the map of human possibility. There are three kinds of people, he says: the normal, the abnormal, and the supernormal. Western psychology, born in the clinic, has only studied the abnormal - the person who has fallen below the norm - and so the whole of Freud's framework rests on sick patients. Eastern psychology watches a different population: the supernormal, the person who has gone beyond the norm. Both deviate from average, but one downward and one upward. Tantra exists to map that upward direction, and these three sutras - light rising the spine, lightning between centres, the cosmos as translucent presence - belong to that ascent. As Osho puts it: "One who is supernormal is abnormal because he is more healthy than any normal human being."
chapters.quote: One who is supernormal is abnormal because he is more healthy than any normal human being.

chapters.title: Conscious Doing
chapters.summary: Osho opens with a piercing observation: when he looks into people's eyes, no one is really there - they exist absently. This absence, he says, is the root of all human suffering. Whatever you do, if done with full presence, becomes ecstasy; if done in absence, becomes hell. So there are two kinds of seeker - one obsessed with what to do, one focused on how to be. Only the second is on the right path. He retells Buddha turning back a man who wanted to serve the world: "You cannot do anything because you are not. First be." These three sutras (the clear summer sky, space absorbed in your head, knowing yourself as light) bring you to presence rather than to action. As Osho puts it: "Hell means your absence."
chapters.quote: Hell means your absence.

chapters.title: Coming Back to Existence
chapters.summary: Osho opens with a folk joke: a village postmaster meets a "Doctor of Philosophy" and replies, "I have never heard of any case of this disease here." The joke, he says, is nearer to the truth than we think - philosophy is a kind of disease, abstract thinking that moves in circles and never lands. Science and religion both reach answers because both rest on experiment; only philosophy speculates without experimenting and concludes nothing. Religion is the deeper science because the experimenter himself is the experiment. The warning is sharp: the mind will gladly turn even meditation into another topic to think "about." Don't read about meditation - do it. As Osho puts it: "Life's problems can be solved only when you become deeply rooted in existence."
chapters.quote: Life's problems can be solved only when you become deeply rooted in existence.

chapters.title: From Death to Deathlessness
chapters.summary: All enlightened ones, Osho says, agree on one thing only: the ego is the single barrier between you and reality. Trying to drop it is unnecessary - it is a shadow of inattention; bring light to it and it disappears like darkness in a lit room. He gives a usable test: when you are truly silent inside, you exist but no "I" is felt. The instant you fall into anger or desire, the I crystallises again. The ego is the past mind organising itself at the periphery of consciousness - memory pretending to be a self. That is why love and meditation, both ego-dissolving, are the same phenomenon. As Osho puts it: "If you know love, there is no need to know God - you have known him already."
chapters.quote: If you know love, there is no need to know God - you have known him already.

chapters.title: Only the Unreal Dissolves
chapters.summary: Osho opens with an anecdote: a small-town mayor finds out that the priest, the gas-station attendant and the barber all think he is a bum - yet the mayor himself believes he holds the office for its honour. That, he says, is exactly the ego's situation. Only you take your ego seriously; for everyone else it is irrelevant, and reality itself cannot support it because reality is one and the ego pretends to be separate. So we avoid reality, build a glasshouse of dreams, and live inside it. Deep sleep, where the I disappears, is why mornings feel fresh; dream-filled nights leave you tired because the I persists. As Osho puts it: "You are just a wave. The wave comes and goes; the ocean is not worried about it."
chapters.quote: You are just a wave. The wave comes and goes; the ocean is not worried about it.

chapters.title: You Are Everywhere
chapters.summary: Osho opens with a folk story: an old doctor, told of a patient choking on a billiard ball, says "tickle him with a feather" - the patient laughs, the ball flies out. The doctor's rule of life is "when you don't know what to do, do something." That rule, Osho says, is fatal for meditation. The mind is the most delicate mechanism in existence; mixing techniques or modifying them is dangerous, and a wrong move is hard to undo. These methods are revolutionary - shortcuts that compress millions of evolutionary years into a single jump. The first sutra targets body-attachment. You've been embodied for so many lives that you only really know yourself as a body; anything else is borrowed reading. "Non-doing will be more beneficial to you than any doing."
chapters.quote: Non-doing will be more beneficial to you than any doing.

chapters.title: Watch from the Hill
chapters.summary: Osho describes man as Janus-faced: animal in his past, divine in his potential future, living as a tense step between the two. Whatever side you satisfy, the other side rebels. He illustrates with a joke - a Jaguar enthusiast finds heaven has beautiful highways but allows no cars, while hell has cars but no highways. That, says Osho, is our predicament exactly. Religions take sides - they fight the animal for the divine - and so create the very split they pretend to heal. Tantra refuses both sides and only watches. By staying in the present, neither shamed by the past nor reaching for the future, the duality dissolves into a third position. As Osho puts it: "Tantra is in the present. It is neither past nor future."
chapters.quote: Tantra is in the present. It is neither past nor future.

chapters.title: Techniques to Become One with the Whole
chapters.summary: Osho opens with Lord Mancroft cutting a speech short because his house is literally on fire. Then the punchline: your house too is on fire, but you don't seem the least bit flustered. Death is approaching every moment, life is shrinking, but the mind whispers there's plenty of time. He tells the devil's parable to drive the point home: in a hellish recession, the most cunning disciple proposed not to deny religion or hell but only to convince humans that "there is no hurry" - and from that day hell has had an overpopulation problem. Without crisis-level urgency, no technique can work. Understanding must become action; mere acquaintance is useless. As Osho puts it: "Your house is also on fire, but you don't even seem a little flustered."
chapters.quote: Your house is also on fire, but you don't even seem a little flustered.

chapters.title: Start Creating Yourself
chapters.summary: Osho opens with a joke - a stranger at the back of a dull church is mistaken for a board member; he replies he must be the most bored man there. From the joke he turns serious: boredom is uniquely human. Animals feel pain but not anguish; for them suffering is momentary, an accident on the periphery, never the centre. Only humans carry pain forward into a constant wound, and only humans commit suicide. Aristotle called man the rational animal, but rationality is only a matter of degree - boredom is the cleaner definition. Hope is what stops most people from suicide; he tells the prime-minister-and-flying-horse story to show how a thin "maybe" keeps people dragging on. These techniques aim past hope, at real celebration. As Osho puts it: "Animals can be in pain, but they are not in suffering."
chapters.quote: Animals can be in pain, but they are not in suffering.

chapters.title: Destroy the Limits
chapters.summary: Osho draws a sharp distinction: life is not a problem, it is a mystery. Science treats life as a problem and applies the mind to it; religion treats it as a mystery and asks for your whole self. A problem can be solved; a mystery can only be lived. That is why a brilliant scientist can be a fool in his own life - the mind sharpens within his speciality but cannot meet the wider mystery, which requires the whole. Religion has no answers, only doorways. These two short sutras - placing mind-stuff in fineness above, below and in the heart, and feeling some part of your form as limitlessly spacious - open one such doorway. As Osho puts it: "Life is not a problem but a mystery."
chapters.quote: Life is not a problem but a mystery.

chapters.title: Go Beyond Mind and Matter
chapters.summary: Osho frames these techniques with a long-running philosophical fight: is mind or matter the basic stuff of the universe? Materialists like Charvak, Epicurus and Marx pick matter; idealists like the Vedantins pick mind. Modern physics has dissolved both - Eddington said the universe looks more like a thought than a thing. Tantra has always taken a third position: matter and mind are two forms of an unnamed X. The practical payoff is that you can enter reality through either door - Hatha Yoga works through the body, Raja Yoga through the mind, and Tantra uses both. This is why Shiva's techniques sometimes ask you to do something physical and sometimes only to imagine. For Tantra, even imagination is real. "Even imagination is a mode of reality because the mind is a manifestation of reality."
chapters.quote: Even imagination is a mode of reality because the mind is a manifestation of reality.

chapters.title: You Are Unknown to Yourself
chapters.summary: Osho opens with a stark statement: man is born alone and dies alone, and aloneness is therefore his basic reality - society is only accidental. Yet between birth and death we live so completely in others that the self we know is just labels they have given us: a name, a reputation, a moral image. No one is really known to themselves. That, he says, is the root of human anxiety, and no theory about "soul eternal" can cure it because that knowledge too is borrowed. He turns to Mahavira, who lived twelve years silent and naked in the forest - the real nakedness, Osho says, was the stripping of every social label. These two sutras open the same door. "Aloneness is his basic reality; society is just accidental."
chapters.quote: Aloneness is his basic reality; society is just accidental.

chapters.title: Forget the Periphery
chapters.summary: Osho describes the surface of life as a cyclone - but the cyclone isn't life itself; deep down there is a silent centre. The trouble is identification with the surface, which keeps us mad. He tells the Sufi story: a fakir clings all night to a branch over what he believes is a bottomless abyss; when his frozen hands finally fail and he drops, he lands on solid ground just below. The whole ordeal was unnecessary. That, Osho says, is our condition exactly - we cling to the periphery, terrified of an inner abyss that is in fact the ground of our being. Tantra is letting go of the surface and falling backwards into oneself. "Meditation is the deepest sleep. It is total relaxation plus something more."
chapters.quote: Meditation is the deepest sleep. It is total relaxation plus something more.

chapters.title: Fear of Transformation Goes Deep
chapters.summary: Osho confronts a hard fact: many are interested in meditation, very few are transformed by it. The reason, he says, is fear - the fear of transformation, which is essentially the fear of death. The old self must go before the new can come, and we don't really want that. So the mind cunningly manufactures a superficial interest, lots of reading and discussing, while the real intensity is missing. The trick: even a wrong method works if you are deeply involved, and the right method fails if you are only deceiving yourself. He tells the Mulla story - the schoolhouse is on fire and Mulla is praying for rain, calling that "doing something." Prayer too can become a hideout. As Osho puts it: "You want to be transformed, but simultaneously you want to remain the old."
chapters.quote: You want to be transformed, but simultaneously you want to remain the old.

chapters.title: Seek the Rhythm of Opposites
chapters.summary: Osho opens with a Walt Whitman line: "I contradict myself because I am big, because I contain all the opposites." The same, he says, can be said of Shiva and of Tantra. Tantra is the search for the rhythm of opposites: male and female, day and night, positive and negative, life and death. The river of life flows between two banks that look contradictory but together hold one current. That is why these one hundred and twelve techniques are so different and sometimes even diametrically opposed - they are not wrong; they are different banks for different temperaments. These four sutras work that way, weaving inner and outer, desire and silence, particular and universal into one rhythm. As Osho puts it: "Tantra is the search for the rhythm of opposites."
chapters.quote: Tantra is the search for the rhythm of opposites.

chapters.title: Become Each Being
chapters.summary: Osho roots these techniques in a single insight: existence is one, but self-consciousness gives each of us the false feeling of being a separate centre, and that false separation is the source of every anxiety, every fear of death. In deep sleep we lose this separation and merge back into the whole - that is why mornings feel reborn. Samadhi, he says, is just sleep done consciously: Buddha goes nightly to the same source you visit in dreamless sleep, only he goes awake. The ego, like an eggshell, has its protective use early on, but eventually it must dissolve to let the inner being sprout. These three sutras - feeling each consciousness as your own, becoming each being - work directly to crack that shell. As Osho puts it: "Hindus have always believed that samadhi is conscious sleep."
chapters.quote: Hindus have always believed that samadhi is conscious sleep.

chapters.title: The Philosophy of Emptiness
chapters.summary: Osho closes the book with the most delicate techniques of all - those of emptiness. He explains that Buddha used these very four sutras for his bhikkhus, and was so badly misunderstood that Buddhism was uprooted from Indian soil. Buddha denied God, denied soul, denied any substantial goal - not because he was an atheist, but to clear the ground for total emptiness. Earlier masters had told seekers to "be desireless" while still dangling moksha as the prize, which Osho calls a basic contradiction. Buddha removed even the prize so desirelessness could finally be real. The result is not nothing - it is a vacant room in which existence can speak. As Osho puts it: "You can enter the truth only when you are totally empty."
chapters.quote: You can enter the truth only when you are totally empty.
